items.q: Are claims that women use more 'tentative' language well-supported?
items.correct: Early studies (Lakoff) made broad claims, but later research (e.g. Holmes, Cameron) shows context matters more than gender, and many supposed differences disappear with proper data.
items.misconception: Women always use more hedges and tag questions than men.
items.random_wrong: Women and men have different vocal cords so speak differently.
items.why: Stereotypes about gendered language are widely repeated in textbooks but contested by current research. Students inherit Lakoff's claims without the critique that has followed.

items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.q: What is Skinner's behaviourist view of language acquisition?
items.correct: Children learn language through imitation and operant conditioning — they are reinforced for correct utterances and corrected for errors.
items.misconception: Skinner believed in an innate grammar module.
items.random_wrong: Skinner believed children couldn't learn language until age five.
items.why: Skinner is the BEHAVIOURIST — the opposite of nativists like Chomsky. Students mix up the two. Skinner's view is environmental; Chomsky's is innate.

items.q: Which model is associated with Zimmerman and West?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dominance. “Deficit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Robin Lakoff is associated with the:
items.why: It's Deficit model. “Dominance model” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many of the four classic gender-and-language models have been outlined here?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
